summary: Simon Späti built a personal knowledge assistant using RAG on his Obsidian vault, with DuckDB as the vector database locally and MotherDuck for a serverless web app via WASM. The system uses BGE-M3 embeddings to enable semantic search across nearly 9,000 markdown notes, leveraging Obsidian's backlink graph to boost results and surface hidden connections between notes. He emphasizes the local-first approach to keep sensitive notes private. The article doubles as a reflection on AI agents in data engineering, arguing that 'plan mode' in tools like Claude Code is the real productivity breakthrough — not smarter models. He cautions that vibe coding and AI agents still require a human architect to avoid generating unmaintainable code that solves the wrong problem.
key_points: DuckDB works surprisingly well as a combined relational and vector database, handling embeddings and cosine similarity in a single file without a dedicated vector DB
The real AI productivity boost comes from plan mode — structured planning before execution — not from models getting smarter
Local-first RAG preserves privacy and gives better results because the context is your own curated notes, not generic internet data
Graph-boosted search that combines semantic similarity with Obsidian's backlink structure surfaces better results than either approach alone
Vibe coding works best when extending an existing framework or skeleton, not starting from scratch — config-driven development with a human architect in the loop
money_quotes: I was surprised how well DuckDB handles this without a dedicated vector database, one file for relational data and vectors together.
The hidden connections this tool surfaces are valuable only because they're my connections, my thinking, not just crawled information on the internet.
Let them run without direction, and you'll get a thousand lines solving the wrong problem.
I get the perception of being super productive, but after a couple of hours, or sometimes days, we actually didn't achieve what we needed.
The character and soul of the person gets stripped away. The quirky things someone does, which make them who they are, that takes away from the fun of writing.
Always keep it simple, because it's easy to make it complex. The true beauty lies in making it simple, which is something agents are not good at.
Human in the seat and config-driven development is what it comes down to with AI agents.
The hard part is coming up with the spec, talking to business, etc. The coding part is the small part.
key_insight: AI agents accelerate building but the real productivity unlock is plan mode — structured human-in-the-loop planning before autonomous execution — not smarter models.
